one_line_takeaway: A hypothesis-testing framework that, via a pivotal statistic and a minimax efficiency program, yields provably optimal closed-form detectors for the Gumbel-max and inverse-transform LLM watermarks, showing OpenAI's Aaronson rule is suboptimal and characterizing exactly when it beats the log-score alternative.
key_finding: Aaronson's widely-used Gumbel-max detector h_ars(r)=-log(1-r) (implemented internally at OpenAI) is SUBOPTIMAL in class-dependent efficiency; the paper derives the strictly optimal closed-form rule h*_gum,Delta. It further proves (Theorem 3.3) a phase transition at an absolute constant Delta* ~= 0.17756: for 0.001<Delta<Delta*, h_ars beats the alternative h_log(r)=log r; for Delta*<Delta<0.99, h_log beats h_ars; both are dominated by h*_gum,Delta everywhere. On ChatGPT-3.5-turbo NTP data, 56.85% of distributions have top-token probability above 1-Delta* ~= 0.82, i.e. in the regime where h_ars > h_log, explaining prior empirical observations. For the inverse-transform watermark, a surprising large-vocabulary limit result shows Y_dif's H1 law depends essentially only on the top token probability, yielding a closed-form optimal score h*_dif,Delta that attains (asymptotically) infinite power because Y_dif has different support under H0 vs H1 as |W|->infinity.
core_contribution: The paper develops a general statistical (hypothesis-testing) framework for analyzing and designing DETECTION RULES for LLM watermarks, and uses it to derive provably optimal detectors for two representative unbiased watermarks. Its own contributions are: (1) a 'pivotal statistic' construction Y_t = Y(w_t, zeta_t) whose null distribution mu_0 is identical for every next-token-prediction (NTP) distribution P_t, giving provable Type I (false-positive) control WITHOUT knowing the (unavailable) NTP distributions; (2) a closed-form asymptotic Type II (false-negative) error via large-deviation theory, formalized as a 'class-dependent efficiency rate' R_P(h) = -inf_{theta>=0} sup_{P in P}(theta E_0 h(Y) + log phi_{P,h}(theta)); (3) reduction of finding the most powerful detector to a minimax program min_h max_P L(h,P) with L(h,P)=E_0 h(Y)+log E_{1,P} e^{-h(Y)}; and (4) closed-form OPTIMAL score functions for the Gumbel-max watermark (h*_gum,Delta) and the inverse-transform watermark (h*_dif,Delta), each maximizing the P_Delta-efficiency over the 'Delta-regular' distribution class P_Delta = {P : max_w P_w <= 1-Delta}. A key technical device is a Convexity Lemma showing P -> phi_h(P) is convex for the Gumbel-max watermark, so the worst-case P is attained at a vertex (the least-favorable distribution P*_Delta) of P_Delta.
threat_model: Content-provenance / AI-generated-text detection. The verifier holds a secret key and must decide H0 (text written by a human) vs H1 (text produced by a watermarked LLM), formalized as hypothesis testing on the token sequence w_{1:n} and derived pseudorandom variables zeta_{1:n}=A(w_{1:t-1}, Key). Motivations the paper cites: curbing misinformation/bots, academic dishonesty, and training-data pollution. Two 'working hypotheses' underpin the guarantees: soundness of pseudorandomness (zeta_t i.i.d. and independent of prior tokens, justified cryptographically) and intrinsic human randomness (human token independent of zeta_t). NOTE: this is a DETECTION-EFFICIENCY threat model, not an adversarial-robustness one. The paper does not model an attacker who edits, paraphrases, or strips the watermark; robustness to such attacks is out of scope for its own results.
limitations: (1) Scope is unbiased watermarks (Gumbel-max, inverse transform); the popular biased red-green-list watermark of Kirchenbauer et al. is explicitly framed as prior/other work and is NOT analyzed. (2) Guarantees rest on the two working hypotheses (cryptographic pseudorandomness soundness; human-token independence) which are assumed, not empirically tested. (3) Optimality is asymptotic (large sequence length n; Type II bound is a large-deviation limit) and defined relative to a chosen distribution class P_Delta, so the 'right' Delta must be picked; if nothing is known about the LLM (full simplex) efficiency is zero. (4) The inverse-transform optimal rule is derived under a single-dominant-token / large-|W| asymptotic (belief class P_bar_Delta requiring the second-largest probability to vanish, log|W|*eps_|W| -> 0). (5) No adversarial robustness: paraphrasing/edit attacks and watermark removal are not modeled in the paper's own analysis.

datasets_used: Synthetic token sequences with vocabulary |W|=1,000 (spike/NTP distributions: P_(1)=1-Delta_t, Delta_t ~ U[0.001,0.5], remaining mass uniform)
C4 dataset (Raffel et al. [53]) with the OPT-1.3B model for real-text Type I/II evaluation
OPT-1.3B (|W|=50,272) and Sheared-LLaMA-2.7B language models for real watermarked/unwatermarked text
ChatGPT-3.5-turbo NTP top-token probabilities collected from 20 prompts (4,997 top-one probabilities) for the empirical Delta-regularity study
quantitative_results: Phase-transition constant Delta* ~= 0.17756 (unique root of R_{P_Delta}(h_ars)=R_{P_Delta}(h_log)); numerically 0.17756080525...
Synthetic Type I error stays ~0.04-0.06 around nominal alpha=0.05 over 5,000 repetitions (except h_ind,delta which reaches 0.05 only for sequences >300 tokens)
Real-LLM (OPT-1.3B on C4) Type I error within 0.04-0.06 near nominal 0.05, especially for texts >100 tokens, over 500 repetitions
Empirical Type II ranking matches Theorem 3.3: h_ars < h_log < h_ind,e^-1 (higher = worse), with h*_gum,Delta and h*_dif,Delta lowest in both synthetic and real experiments
ChatGPT-3.5-turbo NTP: 81.36% of top-one probabilities < 0.999; 99.79% > 0.001; 56.85% of distributions have top-token prob above 1-Delta* ~= 0.82
Delta-regularity of ChatGPT-3.5-turbo NTP distributions: 88.65% are 0.0001-regular, 81.36% are 0.001-regular, 70.28% are 0.01-regular
Inverse-transform limiting laws (Theorem 4.1, |W|->inf): H0 CDF -> 1-(1-r)^2; H1 CDF -> 1-(1-r/(1-Delta))^2 on r in [0,1-Delta]
baselines_compared: h_ars(r) = -log(1-r): Aaronson's Gumbel-max detector, internally used at OpenAI [1]
h_neg: negative-difference score for the inverse-transform watermark [38, 50]

Composite-independence-testing detector of [31] (contrasted qualitatively: non-vanishing worst-case Type II error and exponentially large rejection regions vs the paper's vanishing-error, sum-based, computationally efficient rule)
